claim: The microstructure of the duplex lightweight steel consists of austenite and B2 phases, with dual-nanoprecipitation of L'1_2 type long-range ordered domains and VC carbides within the austenite.
evidence_text: The microstructure of duplex lightweight steel consists of austenite and B2 phases, with the dual-nanoprecipitation of L' 1 _ 2 type longrange ordered domains and VC carbides within the austenite.

claim: The plates were homogenized at 1050 °C for 1 h, hot-rolled to 3 mm and water-quenched, then cold-rolled to 1.5 mm with a single-pass reduction ≤5% (total reduction 50%), and finally solution-treated at 900 °C for 15 min followed by water quenching.
evidence_text: These plates were first homogenized at 1050 °C for 1 h and then hotrolled to a thickness of 3 mm. Following hot rolling, the plates were immediately waterquenched to room temperature. The hot-rolled sheets were subsequently cold-rolled to a final thickness of approximately 1.5 mm. The single-pass reduction rate during cold rolling was maintained at or below 5%, corresponding to a total thickness reduction of 50%. Then, the cold-rolled sheet was solution-treated at 900 °C for 15 min, followed by water quenching

claim: According to the equilibrium phase diagram (Figure 1b), the duplex lightweight steel at 900 °C consists of austenite and B2 phases with VC precipitation.
evidence_text: Figure 1b displays the equilibrium phase diagram of the duplex lightweight steel calculated using Thermo-Calc 2021b software, which indicates the coexistence of austenite and B2 phases at 900 °C, along with the precipitation of VC.

claim: Uniaxial tensile tests used flat dog-bone-shaped specimens with gauge dimensions of 20 × 5 × 1.5 mm³ and a strain rate of 1 × 10⁻³ s⁻¹.
evidence_text: Flat dog-bone-shaped tensile specimens with a gauge dimension of 20 × 5 × 1.5 mm³ were cut from the solution-treated steel plate by electrical discharge machining (EDM). Uniaxial tensile tests were conducted at a strain rate of 1 × 10⁻³ s⁻¹

claim: Cyclic loading-unloading-reloading (LUR) tensile tests were performed at a loading and unloading rate of 1 × 10⁻³ s⁻¹.
evidence_text: In addition, cyclic loading-unloading-reloading (LUR) tensile tests were performed on the same apparatus at a loading and unloading rate of 1 × 10⁻³ s⁻¹.

claim: In situ tensile tests used dog-bone specimens with gauge dimensions 8 × 2 × 1 mm³, a tensile rate of 2 µm/s, and were interrupted at strains of 0%, 1%, 3%, and 6% for EBSD data acquisition.
evidence_text: Dog-bone-shaped specimens with a gauge dimension of 8 × 2 × 1 mm³ were prepared via EDM for these tests. The tensile rate was maintained at 2 µm/s, and tests were paused at tensile strains of 0%, 1%, 3%, and 6% to acquire EBSD data of the same surface region.

claim: XRD analysis used Cu-Kα radiation with a scanning range from 20° to 100° and a scanning rate of 2° min⁻¹.
evidence_text: The XRD analysis was conducted with a Bruker D8 Focus diffractometer using Cu-Kα radiation with a scanning range from 20° to 100° and a scanning rate of 2° min⁻¹.

claim: EBSD data were acquired with a sample tilt angle of 70°, an acceleration voltage of 20 kV, a working distance of 15 mm, and a step size of 0.1 µm.
evidence_text: The following settings were used during EBSD data acquisition: a sample tilt angle of 70°, an acceleration voltage of 20 kV, a working distance of 15 mm, and step sizes of 0.1 µm.

claim: TEM foils were prepared by grinding to <100 µm thickness followed by double-jet electrochemical polishing at -30 °C and 20 V using an electrolyte of 90 vol.% methanol and 10 vol.% perchloric acid.
evidence_text: were first ground into foils with a thickness of <100 µm and then further thinned until perforation by double-jet electrochemical polishing at 20 V and -30 °C with an electrolyte consisting of 90 vol.% methanol and 10 vol.% perchloric acid.

claim: TEM observation was performed using a Thermofisher Talos F200X instrument operated at 200 kV, and local nanoscale chemical compositions were measured via STEM-EDX.
evidence_text: TEM observation was carried out in Thermofisher Talos F200X instrument operated at 200 kV. The local nanoscale chemical compositions were measured through energy dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) in the scanning transmission electron microscopy (STEM) mode.

claim: XRD analysis of the lightweight steel identified diffraction peaks of austenite, B2, and VC precipitation, and quantified the B2 phase fraction as 20%.
evidence_text: Figure 2a presents the XRD pattern of the lightweight steel, in which diffraction peaks corresponding to the austenite, B2 phases, and VC precipitation are clearly identified… revealing a B2 phase fraction (V_B2) of 20%.

claim: IPF maps (Figure 2c,d) reveal that austenite has an equiaxed morphology, whereas B2 appears in two distinct forms: long strip–like large grains and fine particles decorating austenite grain boundaries.
evidence_text: The inverse pole figure (IPF) maps (Figure 2c,d) reveal that the austenite exhibits an equiaxed morphology, whereas the B2 phase appears in two distinct forms, including long strip–like large grains and fine particles decorating austenite grain boundaries.

claim: The average grain size of austenite is 1.31 µm, while B2 exhibits a bimodal size distribution with mean grain sizes of 1.02 µm and 3.38 µm, based on EBSD grain-size frequency distributions (Figure 2e,f).
evidence_text: The grain-size frequency distributions for the two phases are shown in Figure 2e,f, indicating an average grain size of 1.31 µm for austenite and a bimodal size distribution for B2, with mean grain sizes of 1.02 µm and 3.38 µm.

claim: TEM bright-field image statistics give an average VC precipitate size of 19.9 ± 6.2 nm and a volume fraction of 0.26%.
evidence_text: The statistics of VC precipitation on several bright-field (BF) TEM images showed that the average size of VC was 19.9 ± 6.2 nm with a volume fraction of 0.26%.

claim: SADP along the [011]γ zone axis shows superlattice reflections originating from nanoscale L'1_2-type long-range ordered domains inside austenite, and dark-field TEM (Figure 3d) reveals these domains as homogeneously distributed nanoscale particles.
evidence_text: The inset of Figure 3d displays the selected area diffraction pattern (SADP) taken along the [011]γ zone axis … additional superlattice reflections are visible. … The dark-field (DF) TEM image taken from the superlattice reflections … L'1_2 ordered domains … appear as homogeneously distributed nanoscale particles.

claim: Inverse FFT image (Figure 3e) reveals that the L'1_2 ordered domains have irregular shapes, unlike cuboid-shaped κ'-carbides.
evidence_text: The inverse FFT image (Figure 3e) further reveals that the L'1_2 ordered domains exist as LRO domains with irregular shapes rather than cuboid-shaped κ'-carbides [22].

claim: Statistical analysis shows an average L'1_2 LRO domain size of 1.0 ± 0.3 nm and a volume fraction of 11.10%.
evidence_text: Statistical results reveal an average LRO domain size of 1.0 ± 0.3 nm and a volume fraction of 11.10%.

claim: The duplex lightweight steel exhibits a yield strength of 1316 ± 16 MPa, ultimate tensile strength of 1458 ± 11 MPa, and uniform elongation of 11.7 ± 1.2%.
evidence_text: The steel exhibits a combination of ultra-high strength and excellent ductility, where the YS is 1316 ± 16 MPa, the ultimate tensile strength is 1458 ± 11 MPa, and uniform elongation is 11.7 ± 1.2%.

claim: The true stress–strain curve reveals three work-hardening stages: stage I (0–1% true strain) with a sharp decrease to below zero, stage II (1–4.3%) with a rapid increase, and stage III (>4.3%) with a slow decrease but sustained high level of 2.7–3.7 GPa.
evidence_text: three work-hardening stages can be observed. In stage I (0–1%), the work-hardening rate decreases sharply until it is below zero... stage II (1–4.3%) is characterized by the rapid increase in the work-hardening rate. In stage III, exceeding 4.3% true strain, the work-hardening rate slowly decreases but remains at a high level (2.7 to 3.7 GPa)

claim: The hetero-deformation induced (HDI) stress is calculated as σ_HDI = (σ_u + σ_r)/2, where σ_u is the unloaded yield stress and σ_r is the reloaded yield stress.
evidence_text: the HDI stress can be calculated using the equation σ_HDI = (σ_u + σ_r)/2, as proposed by Yang et al.

claim: Both the hysteresis loop area and the yield drop Δσ_y increase with true strain; at true strains of 1%, 3%, 5%, 7%, 9%, and 11% the hysteresis area values are 70, 115, 155, 162, 168, and 202 MPa, and Δσ_y values are 2, 8, 13, 15, 20, and 23 MPa.
evidence_text: The results show that the hysteresis area increases with increasing strain, effectively reflecting the Bauschinger effect and HDI strengthening. The Δσ_y gradually increases with strain accumulation.

claim: HDI strengthening arises from deformation incompatibility between different grains and phases, which induces accumulation of geometrically necessary dislocations at heterogeneous interfaces, generating long-range back stresses in soft regions and forward stresses in hard regions, thus enhancing resistance to plastic deformation.
evidence_text: This is primarily attributed to the deformation incompatibility between different grains and phases, which induces the accumulation of GNDs at heterogeneous interfaces, generating long-range back stresses and forward stresses in the soft and hard regions, respectively. Finally, the concurrent development of back and forward stresses enhances the resistance to plastic deformation, resulting in HDI strengthening and work-hardening.

claim: The yield stress (σ_YS) of the duplex lightweight steel is decomposed into contributions from austenite (σ_γ f_γ), B2 phase (σ_B2 f_B2), and hetero-deformation induced (HDI) strengthening (σ_HDI) according to Eq. (1).
evidence_text: Thus, the contribution of different strengthening mechanisms to the yield stress (σ_YS) can be calculated from the following equation: σ_YS = σ_γ f_γ + σ_B2 f_B2 + σ_HDI

claim: The austenite yield stress (σ_γ) consists of intrinsic lattice friction (σ_0,γ = 97 MPa), solid solution strengthening (σ_ss,γ), dislocation forest strengthening (σ_dis,γ), VC precipitation strengthening (σ_VC,γ), and LRO ordering strengthening (σ_LRO,γ) as in Eq. (2).
evidence_text: σ_0,γ is 97 MPa [17].

claim: The B2 phase yield stress (σ_B2) consists of intrinsic lattice friction (σ_0,B2 = 676 MPa) and dislocation forest strengthening (σ_dis,B2) as in Eq. (3).
evidence_text: σ_0,B2 and σ_dis,B2 correspond to the contributions to YS from the intrinsic lattice friction stress and dislocation forest strengthening in B2. The σ0 of the austenite and B2 phase are 97 MPa and 676 MPa, respectively [26].

claim: The solid solution strengthening contribution (σ_ss,γ) in the austenite is calculated using the empirical equation σ_ss,γ = 91.3 X_C - 1.5 X_Mn + 20.5 X_Al + 2.9 X_Ni + 3 X_V, yielding 228 MPa.
evidence_text: σ_ss,γ = 9 1. 3 X_C - 1. 5 X_Mn + 2 0. 5 X_Al + 2. 9 X_Ni + 3 X_V (4) ... The calculated σ_ss,γ is 228 MPa.

claim: The dislocation strengthening (σ_dis) follows the Taylor model σ_dis = M α G b √ρ; using EBSD GND densities, the calculated σ_dis for austenite is 106 MPa and for B2 is 227 MPa.
evidence_text: Based on the average GND densities obtained from EBSD data (Figure 7), the calculated values of σ_dis for austenite and B2 are 106 and 227 MPa, respectively.

claim: The hetero-deformation induced (HDI) strengthening contribution at yielding, obtained by extrapolating the HDI stress–strain curve in Figure 5d, is 597 MPa.
evidence_text: σ_HDI can be obtained by extrapolating the HDI stress–strain curve (Figure 5d) [27], and the value of σ_HDI is 597 MPa.

claim: The strengthening contribution from L'1_2 long-range ordered domains (σ_LRO) is estimated by the difference between the measured YS and the sum of other calculated contributions, yielding 119 MPa.
evidence_text: σ_LRO can be calculated by the difference between the YS and the other strengthening contributions, which is estimated to be 119 MPa.

claim: In stage I (0 to ~1% strain), the work-hardening rate decreases sharply due to a large dislocation mean free path and further drops below zero after yielding of the hard B2 phase owing to rapid relaxation of internal stress and strain at the austenite/B2 interface.
evidence_text: At stage I, the rate of workhardening decreases sharply due to the large dislocation mean free path in the austenite and B2 grains (Figure 6(a1,a2,b1,b2)). The rate further decreases to below zero due to the rapid relaxation of internal stress and strain at the austenite/B2 interface after the yielding of the hard B2 phase [17].

claim: During stage II (1–4.3% true strain), the work-hardening rate rises rapidly and attains a maximum near 4.3% true strain, driven by rapid dislocation storage in both phases and pronounced accumulation at austenite/B2 interfaces, leading to robust forest dislocation hardening and hetero-deformation induced (HDI) hardening.
evidence_text: At stage II, the work-hardening rate increases rapidly with strain, attaining a maximum value near 4.3% true strain. This pronounced ascent stems in part from the rapid storage of dislocations within both the austenite and B2 grains (Figure 6(e,f)), coupled with their pronounced accumulation at austenite/B2 interfaces (Figure 6(c1,c2)), which together engender robust forest dislocation hardening. Furthermore, heterogeneous distribution of dislocations within grains and at austenite/B2 interfaces contributes to substantial HDI hardening (Figure 5d).

claim: The Fe-30Mn-9Al-1C-1V-5Ni duplex lightweight steel, comprising austenite and B2 phases, can be produced by simple rolling and heat treatment, with L'1_2-type LRO domains and VC nanoprecipitation present in the austenite.
evidence_text: This duplex lightweight steel, comprising austenite and B2 phases, can be produced through simple rolling and heat treatment. L'1_2 type LRO domains and VC nanoprecipitation are present within the austenite phase.

claim: The duplex lightweight steel exhibits a yield strength of 1316±16 MPa, tensile strength of 1458±11 MPa, and total elongation of 11.7±1.2%, with three work-hardening stages and a final work-hardening rate of 2.7–3.7 GPa.
evidence_text: This duplex lightweight steel demonstrates exceptional strength and ductility, with a YS of 1316±16 MPa, a tensile strength of 1458±11 MPa, and a total elongation of 11.7±1.2%. It exhibits three discernible stages of work-hardening, characterized by a work-hardening rate that initially decreases and then increases, and finally remains at a relatively high level (2.7 to 3.7 GPa) with a slight decrease.
